skill.name: projection-patterns
skill.description: Guides implementation of CQRS read models, materialized views, search indexes, and aggregated projections from event streams.
skill.summary: Build reliable event-sourced projections and read models for query workloads.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network indicators, but review found these are documentation false positives. The skill contains Markdown diagrams, Python examples, SQL templates, and two resource links, with no executable installer scripts or prompt injection attempts found.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown code fences flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The backtick findings are Markdown code block fences around diagrams and Python examples, not Ruby or shell execution. They do not execute commands when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are Markdown fences that delimit diagrams or sample Python code. No shell command, Ruby backtick expression, or execution context is present.

low_findings.title: False positive: weak cryptography substring matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary words such as description, Description, and Design. No hashing, cipher selection, or cryptographic API usage appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain plain prose or data field names. There is no evidence of MD5, DES, SHA-1, or any cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: False positive: SQL WHERE clauses flagged as reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance alerts occur on parameterized SQL WHERE clauses inside read-model examples. They query application tables and do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is an SQL predicate using placeholders such as $1. The code examples do not call operating system discovery commands or enumerate local resources.

low_findings.title: Documentation links to external resources
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for runtime network risk. The hardcoded URLs are resource links in a Markdown references section. They are not fetched by code and do not transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references only. No script, request call, or automatic network behavior is present in the skill file.

content.value_statement: Event-sourced systems often need fast query models without weakening the write model. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reusable projection patterns for CQRS read sides.
actual_capabilities: Explains live, catchup, persistent, and inline projection types.
Provides Python templates for projectors and projection handlers.
Shows order summary, product search, daily sales, and customer activity examples.
Demonstrates checkpointing and replay-oriented projection design.
Covers practical guidance for idempotency, ordering, transactions, and rebuilds.
limitations: Provides examples and guidance, not a packaged runtime library.
Assumes familiarity with CQRS, event sourcing, and backend data stores.
Example code needs adaptation for each event store and schema.
Does not include production monitoring, migration, or deployment scripts.

use_cases.title: Design a CQRS read side
use_cases.description: Map event streams into query-optimized tables with checkpoints and replay support.

use_cases.title: Create reporting projections
use_cases.description: Build aggregate views such as daily sales summaries from historical and live events.

use_cases.title: Review projection architecture
use_cases.description: Assess projection types, consistency tradeoffs, and rebuild strategies before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a projection type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new read model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the projection-patterns skill to recommend a projection type for my event-sourced feature. Explain the tradeoffs for consistency, rebuilds, and query speed.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a read model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the projection-patterns skill to design a read model for these events: [paste events]. Include handled event types, fields, and checkpoint strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a projector template
prompt_templates.scenario: Building code from the provided patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the projection-patterns skill to adapt the basic projector template for my stack: [event store], [database], and [runtime]. Keep the design idempotent.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an existing projection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the projection-patterns skill to review this projection design for ordering, replay safety, checkpointing, transaction boundaries, and rebuild risks: [paste design].

output_examples.input: Design a product search projection from product events.
output_examples.output: A recommended search read model with ProductCreated, ProductUpdated, ProductPriceChanged, and ProductDeleted handlers, plus index fields and replay notes.
A checklist covering idempotency, parameterized updates, checkpoint placement, event ordering, and rebuild behavior.

output_examples.input: Create an aggregate projection for daily sales.
output_examples.output: A daily sales projection outline with increment and refund handling, date keys, transaction scope, and consistency considerations.

best_practices: Keep projection handlers idempotent so event replay is safe.
Store checkpoints separately from business data or within the same transaction when consistency requires it.
Design read models around query patterns instead of mirroring write-side aggregates.
anti_patterns: Coupling projections so one read model depends on another read model.
Skipping rebuild planning for projections that must process historical events.
Ignoring event ordering when multiple events update the same read model row.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design and implement projections, read models, search indexes, and aggregate views from event streams.

faq.question: Does it include executable tooling?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with examples and patterns, not a runnable package.

faq.question: Which languages are shown?
faq.answer: The templates use Python examples with async database and search client patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with projection rebuilds?
faq.answer: Yes. It discusses checkpoint deletion, replay, idempotency, and projection design for reconstruction.

faq.question: Is it only for relational databases?
faq.answer: No. It includes relational read models and an Elasticsearch search projection example.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Backend engineers and architects building CQRS or event-sourced systems can use it during design and implementation.
